1: bĕnê hannĕbîʾîm — ‘sons of the prophets.’ The word bēn marks discipleship, not biological relationship: these are members of a prophetic guild attached to Elisha. The plural construct bĕnê identifies a community of trainees, paralleling the guild communities at Bethel and Gilgal (2 Kings 2:3, 2 Kings 4:38). The phrase places 2 Kings 6 firmly within the institutional prophetic tradition that runs from Samuel through Elijah to Elisha.
2: The sons of the prophets request to build a new meeting-place at the Jordan. nillāqaḥ (cohortative, ‘let us take’) expresses collective deliberation and initiative. The detail that the place where they live ‘is too cramped (ṣar) for us’ uses the same root word (ṣārar) used for the constraint of Egypt; the community is outgrowing its space, the opposite of the covenant curse of shrinkage.
3: One of the sons requests Elisha to come with them: hôʾēl nā ûlēk ʾet-ʿăḇādêkā — ‘please be willing to go with your servants.’ hôʾîl is a causative stem of yāʾal, meaning to be willing, to consent, to undertake. Elisha’s one-word answer — ʾēlēk, ‘I will go’ — mirrors the brevity of divine assent elsewhere (cf. the divine lēkā to Moses). The man of God follows his community into ordinary labor.
4: Simple narrative sequence using the waw-consecutive (wayyēlek... wayĕhî... wayyipplû): they went, they came, they felled. The rapid perfective narration (wayyiqṭōl forms) moves the story forward without elaboration. The Jordan is the scene of multiple Elisha miracles; the crossing point of covenant entry (Josh 3) becomes the site of the prophetic community’s building project.
5: ʾăhāh ʾăḏōnî — an interjection of distress: ‘Oh, my master! It was borrowed.’ The key word is šāʾûl (borrowed, from šāʾal): the axe head was a loan. This is not a complaint about the loss alone but a legal anxiety — a borrowed tool that is lost creates an obligation of restitution (Exodus 22:14). The exclamation foregrounds the narrator’s interest in the socioeconomic vulnerability of the prophetic community.
6: Elisha asks, ʾānāh nāpal — ‘where did it fall?’ Then he cuts a stick (ʿēṣ) and throws it at the place — and the iron floats. The verb wayyāṣēp (it floated) is from ṣûp, which means to float or swim; the same root used for the basket in Exodus 2:3 (Moses’s ark). The miracle is accomplished through the cast stick, an ordinary material whose use is not explained — the instrument is instrumental, the power is divine.
7: hārîm lāk — ‘pick it up for yourself.’ The imperative hārîm (lift/raise) with lāk (for yourself, reflexive) assigns the man both the instruction and the benefit. The miracle is not spectacular display but practical restoration — the borrowed iron is returned, the debt is cleared. The economy of grace operates through simple command and simple obedience.
8: melek ʾărām nōḥēm milḥāmāh — ‘the king of Aram was making war.’ The participle nōḥēm (deliberating, planning) from yāʿaṣ — to counsel, to plan. The king ‘counsels with his officers’ (nîʿāṣ ʾel-ʿăḇādāyw) in tactical secrecy, saying ‘at such-and-such a place (māqôm) shall be my camp.’ The king’s secret military strategy is the foil for the prophet who knows all secrets.
9: šālaḥ ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm — ‘the man of God sent word.’ šālaḥ (sent) with no object for the message marks urgency. His warning, hišāmĕr mēʿăḇōr hammāqôm hazzeh — ‘take care not to pass that place’ — uses hišāmēr (guard yourself, beware) with min- (from), the standard warning construction. The information revealed is not from military intelligence but from divine revelation — the narration has the king of Israel discover that Elisha’s intelligence is prophetic, not human (v12).
10: The king of Israel sent to the places the man of God warned about and ‘was on his guard there’ — nišmar šām (guarded himself there). The Hebrew is terse; the king’s response is immediate and effective. The narrator notes this happened ‘more than once or twice’ (lōʾ ʾaḥat wĕlōʾ šĕtāyim, ‘not once and not twice’) — a Hebrew idiom for repeated occurrences, underscoring the pattern.
11: wayyiṣṭarab lēb melek ʾărām — ‘the heart of the king of Aram was troubled/stormy.’ The verb sāʿar in Niphal means to be stormy, disturbed, agitated — used of a stormy sea. The lēb (heart) as seat of understanding and decision is troubled because the confidentiality of royal counsel has been breached. The king suspects a traitor among his officers: mî mišelānû ʾel-melek yiśrāʾēl — ‘which of us is for the king of Israel?’
12: The officer’s answer names Elisha: lōʾ ʾăḏōnî hammelek kî-ʾĕlîšāʿ hannāḇîʾ — ‘none, my lord the king, but Elisha the prophet.’ The subsequent description — yagîd lĕmelek yiśrāʾēl ʾet-haddĕḇārîm ʾăšer tĕdabbēr bĕḥadar miškābĕkā — ‘he tells the king of Israel the words you speak in your bedroom.’ The phrase bĕḥadar miškābĕkā (in your bedroom chamber) echoes Ecclesiastes 10:20 and emphasizes the most private possible speech. The prophet’s knowledge is portrayed as total omniscience, an extension of YHWH’s knowing all things (Psalms 139:4).
13: The king commands, lĕkû rĕʾû ʾêkōh hûʾ — ‘go, see where he is.’ The imperative plural and the king’s intent — wĕʾešlaḥ wĕʾeqqāḥeyhû, ‘I will send and seize him’ — use lāqaḥ (take/seize) for arrest. The response: hinnēh bĕḏōṯān, ‘behold, he is in Dothan.’ Dothan appears in Genesis 37:17 as the place where Joseph was sold by his brothers — the site carries connotations of betrayal and divine rescue.
14: The king sent a great army — sûs wārekeb wĕḥayil kāḇēḏ — ‘horses and chariots and a heavy force.’ The adjective kāḇēḏ (heavy) is used of the Egyptians’ heavy/hard heart at the Exodus and of armies that overwhelm. They arrived lāylāh (at night) and surrounded the city — standard ANE siege preparation under cover of darkness.
15: Elisha’s servant (mĕšārēt, attendant/minister — the same word used of Joshua’s service to Moses, Exodus 24:13) rises early and goes out. wayyiśśāʾ ʾet-ʿênāyw wayyarʾ — ‘and he lifted his eyes and saw.’ The compound idiom nāśāʾ ʿênayim (lift the eyes) introduces visually significant moments throughout the OT (Genesis 13:14; Joshua 5:13). His cry to Elisha: ʾăḥāh ʾăḏōnî mah naʿăśeh, ‘alas, my master, what shall we do?’
16: lōʾ-tîrāʾ kî rabbîm ʾăšer ʾittānû mēʾăšer ʾittām — ‘Do not be afraid, for those who are with us are more than those who are with them.’ The chiasmic comparison (rabbîm ʾăšer ʾittānû vs. ʾăšer ʾittām) mirrors the formula of Numbers 14:9 and Joshua 1:9. The key word is rāb (many, great): the unseen army is numerically greater, though invisible. This is the Hebrew equivalent of Romans 8:31: ‘if God is for us, who can be against us?’ — the numerical calculus of faith.
17: Elisha’s prayer: YHWH pĕqaḥ-nā ʾet-ʿênāyw wĕyirʾeh — ‘LORD, please open his eyes that he may see.’ The verb pāqaḥ (open — specifically of eyes) is used in Genesis 3:5 (the serpent’s promise: ‘your eyes will be opened’) and Genesis 21:19 (Hagar’s eyes opened to see the well). Divine eye-opening is always a gift of sight beyond ordinary seeing. The servant sees sûsîm wĕrekeb ʾēš — ‘horses and chariots of fire.’ The phrase exactly parallels 2 Kings 2:11 (Elijah’s ascent) and brackets the Elisha narrative with fire-chariots at beginning and end of the prophetic succession.
18: Elisha prays a second time: hak nā ʾet-haggôy-hazzeh bassanwĕrîm — ‘please strike this people with blindness/dazzlement.’ The word sanwĕrîm is a hapax legomenon (appears only here and Genesis 19:11, where the angels strike the Sodomites with the same word). It likely derives from a root related to dazzling brightness, not total darkness — a supernatural disorientation of sight. The Arameans are not blinded in the medical sense (they can still walk, following Elisha); they are divinely confused about where they are and who they are following.
19: Elisha’s deception of the disoriented Arameans — lōʾ zeh hadderek wĕlōʾ zōʾt hāʿîr — ‘This is not the way and this is not the city.’ He leads them to Samaria, the capital. The verb ʾōlîk ʾetkem (I will lead you) uses the Hiphil of hālak — causative leading, typically used of God leading his people (the Exodus). Elisha is re-enacting the divine leading of the desert, but to the center of Israel’s power rather than the promised land.
20: On arrival in Samaria, Elisha prays a third time: YHWH pĕqaḥ ʾet-ʿênê ʾēlleh wĕyirʾû — ‘LORD, open the eyes of these men that they may see.’ The same verb pāqaḥ used in v17 creates a double eye-opening: the servant’s eyes opened to see the divine army; the Arameans’ eyes opened to see themselves surrounded. The verse contains an ironic inclusio between v17 and v20: both use ‘open his/their eyes’ to reveal reality, but one revelation is salvation and the other is captivity.
21: The king of Israel’s question: hakkōh ʾakkeh ʾăbî hakkōh ʾakkeh — ‘Shall I strike them, my father? Shall I strike them?’ The doubled question with hakkōh (thus/in this manner) expresses eagerness. The address ʾāḇî (my father) to Elisha is the same address Elisha used for Elijah (2 Kings 2:12); it marks the prophet as a paternal authority over the king. The repetition of the question intensifies both the eagerness and the waiting for prophetic authorization.
22: Elisha’s prohibition: lōʾ takkeh — ‘You shall not strike them.’ His reasoning uses a rhetorical question: ʾăšer šāḇîtā bĕḥarbekā ûbĕqaštĕkā, ‘those you have captured with your sword and your bow.’ The point is that these men were not captured by Israel’s military force at all — God delivered them. Elisha then commands: śîm lāhem leḥem wāmayim wĕyōʾkĕlû wĕyišĕtû, ‘set bread and water before them that they may eat and drink.’ The feeding of an enemy army by prophetic command has no parallel in the OT.
23: wayyikrōt lāhem kĕrāh gĕḏôlāh — ‘he prepared a great feast for them.’ kārāh (to prepare a feast) is used of festive meals in Genesis 31:54 and Proverbs 9:2. The great feast given to enemies recalls the covenant meal (cf. Exodus 24:11, the elders eating before God on Sinai). After eating and drinking, wayyĕšallĕḥēm (he sent them away) — the Piel of šālaḥ, the same verb used of releasing slaves and prisoners. The Aramean incursions then ceased: lōʾ-yāsĕpû ʿôḏ — ‘they came no more.’
24: wayyĕhî ʾaḥărê-kēn — ‘after this.’ The narrator’s transitional formula signals a major scene shift: the Aramean king Ben-hadad (ben-hadad melek ʾărām) musters his entire army (ʾet-kol-maḥănēhû) and goes up to besiege Samaria. The verb wayyiṣōr (laid siege) from ṣûr marks prolonged military pressure. This is the systematic siege that will produce the famine of vv25-33.
25: wayhî rāʿāḇ gāḏôl bĕšōmĕrôn — ‘there was a great famine in Samaria.’ The severity is measured by market prices: ḥōmĕr rōʾš ḥamôr bĕŠmōnîm kesep, ‘a donkey’s head for eighty shekels.’ The donkey is a non-kosher animal (Leviticus 11:4-7), its head the least valuable part. Normal price of a lamb at Passover: approximately 5 shekels. The price escalation represents a factor of over 200: the covenant economy has collapsed entirely, as Deuteronomy 28:52-57 predicted for a siege.
26: wayyiṣʿaq ʾēlāyw ʾiššāh — ‘a woman cried out to him.’ The verb ṣāʿaq (cry out, cry for help) is used throughout the OT for the cry of the oppressed (Exodus 2:23; Judges 3:15). The king is addressed as ʾăḏōnî hammelek (my lord the king), the standard petition formula — the woman is using the formal channels of royal justice.
27: The king’s response uses two Hebrew nouns in a rhetorical pair: min-haggōren ʾô min-hayyāqeb — ‘from the threshing floor or from the winepress?’ These are the two sources of grain and wine — but the king’s bitter question means: if YHWH won’t help you, what source of provision remains? The king has no grain from the floor and no wine from the press during a siege. His question is a confession of helplessness dressed as a rhetorical question.
28: The king asks what is wrong; the woman’s account is a nightmare: her neighbor said tĕnî ʾet-binkê wĕnōklennû hayyôm — ‘give your son so we can eat him today.’ The verb ʾākal (eat) and the causative nātan (give) in this context carry the full weight of Deuteronomy 28:53: ‘you shall eat the offspring of your own body, the flesh of your sons and daughters.’ The covenant curse is not a hyperbole; it is literally enacted.
29: The woman’s account continues: wattōʾmer ûḇannāh bĕneyhā — ‘and on the next day she hid her son.’ The verb ṣāpan (hide, conceal) used here for concealing a child recalls Exodus 2:2-3 (the mother who hid Moses for three months). The reversal is devastating: in Exodus, a mother concealed a child to save him from slaughter; here, a mother conceals a child from a woman who wants to eat him. The Deuteronomic curse has inverted the Exodus rescue.
30: When the king heard the woman’s words, wayyiqraʿ ʾet-bĕḡādāyw — ‘he tore his clothes.’ The royal mourning gesture (cf. 2 Samuel 3:31) expresses anguish and helplessness. The narrator adds that he was passing along the wall — his torn garments were visible to the people below (wĕhāʿām hišqîp wĕhinnēh hassaq, ‘and the people looked, and behold, sackcloth inside’). The king’s private mourning becomes a public spectacle.
31: The king’s oath against Elisha: kōh-yaʿăśeh lî ʾĕlōhîm wĕkōh yôsîp ʾim-yaʿămōd rōʾš ʾĕlîšāʿ — ‘may God deal with me... if the head of Elisha remains on him today.’ The self-imprecatory formula (kōh yaʿăśeh... wĕkōh yôsîp, ‘so may he do and so may he add’) is the standard Hebrew oath formula (cf. 1 Samuel 14:44; Ruth 1:17). The blame directed at Elisha (rather than at YHWH) reflects a pattern of scapegoating the prophet for divine judgment.
32: Elisha is sitting with the elders (zĕqēnîm). He sees the messenger coming: rĕʾîtem kî-šālaḥ ben-hammĕraṣṣēaḥ hazzeh — ‘see how this son of a murderer has sent.’ The phrase ben-hammĕraṣṣēaḥ (son of a murderer) is a Hebrew idiom: bĕn + noun = one characterized by that quality (cf. bĕn-ḥayil, ‘son of valor’ = valiant man). Elisha’s preemptive announcement of the messenger’s arrival demonstrates prophetic foreknowledge a second time in the chapter.
33: The messenger arrives and the king’s despairing words: hinnēh zōʾt hārāʿāh mēʾēt YHWH mah-ʾôḥil lĕYHWH ʿôḏ — ‘this disaster is from YHWH — what further hope is there from YHWH?’ The word rāʿāh (disaster, evil) is the same word used throughout for moral evil and for catastrophe; here it describes the siege and famine as YHWH’s act. The king’s mah-ʾôḥil (what hope?) is a rare verb form (yāḥal, to wait/hope) — the despairing acknowledgment that YHWH is the source of the crisis and the only possible source of relief.
